items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’ve got that nagging feeling again...
Shpak has a tape recorder,
and the ambassador has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Core meaning is mostly conveyed, but the key comic mechanism is slightly weakened. The reference analysis stresses mock-heroic/high-brow theatrical phrasing (“терзают смутные сомнения”) and, crucially, that «у Шпака — …, у посла — …» indicates each person LOST/was deprived of the item (not possession). The translation shifts to a more casual idiom (“nagging feeling”) and uses present possession (“has”), which blurs the detective-like inference and the callback logic (they no longer have those items).

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Hello, Tsar—pleased to meet you.
Tsar—pleased to meet you, hello.

Tsar—pleased to meet you.
Hello, Tsar—pleased to meet you!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The translation preserves the repetitive, awkwardly polite Soviet-style greeting and the comedic insistence on “Tsar” as a social label. It does not explicitly include “I’m the Tsar,” but the repeated address still communicates the same era-clash humor (banal politeness in a royal setting) and drunken loopiness. Rhythm and redundancy are maintained well for subtitles.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the reference translation; fully preserves the authoritarian, mock-royal performativity and comic escalation.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me be, old woman—I’m in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Very close and functionally equivalent. “Leave me be” is slightly more modern/colloquial than the mock-archaic, high-register melancholy the line carries, but “old woman” + “in sorrow” keeps the comic mismatch (regal lament in a Soviet apartment).

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Meaning is broadly understandable (he feels sealed in), but the English is off in two ways: (1) “Bricked up” is an odd, potentially slangy collocation in modern English and can distract; (2) the Russian implies passive entrapment (“they walled me in”), with “демоны” as the agent; the translation reads more like a label/exclamation than a vivid accusation. The comic ‘mystical interpretation of a mundane malfunction’ is weakened by awkward phrasing.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Accurate and idiomatic enough; preserves the attribution of a mechanical event to religious power. Slightly less elevated/ceremonial than “Behold the power…”, but still conveys the same comic conviction and register contrast.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Communicates the basic directive, but loses the performative, MC-like ‘announcement’ flavor that makes it funnier (a bureaucrat/pretend-tsar issuing a grand proclamation). “Everybody dance!” is serviceable but flatter and more generic than the reference’s ‘floor is open’ framing.

items.translation: What a perfect time to drop in!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Good creative equivalent: conveys opportunistic satisfaction and comic self-congratulation at arriving at the right moment. Tone is light and colloquial, fitting Miloslavsky’s cheerful criminal pragmatism. No meaningful loss of humor.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
Yes! Imported caviar...

Eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: The punchline is preserved (eggplant as the absurd ‘overseas’ delicacy), but the translation keeps calling it “caviar,” which the reference explicitly warns against because it confuses non-Russian viewers (eggplant ‘ikra’ is a spread/relish, not caviar). This is a key cultural joke: the inversion works best when the last item is clearly a cheap spread presented as exotic. As rendered, viewers may think it is literally eggplant caviar, dulling the satire.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
a suede jacket—three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Captures the escalating inventory-list humor and the final “three” beat. Slight nuance: “domestic cigarette cases” is fine, though the original’s hesitating insertion “отечественных” is part of Shpak’s self-important cataloging; the translation keeps it. Overall the comedic accounting effect is intact.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Essentially identical in effect: preserves the calm, sing-song fatalism and the shared ‘we’re all crazy’ punch. Exclamation marks add a bit more energy than the reference ellipses, but do not break the joke.

summary.interpretation: 2.09 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.42 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
